userQuery: What's the probability our coastal property portfolio sees a >$10M annual flood loss by 2035 under current emissions trajectories?

nodes.name: Realized Warming by 2035
nodes.description: Degrees Celsius above pre-industrial expected by 2035. Current IPCC trajectories put us between SSP2-4.5 and SSP3-7.0; policy uncertainty drives the wide range. This is the dominant source of physical-risk variance.

nodes.name: Local Sea-Level Increment (cm)
nodes.description: Sea-level rise above 2020 baseline at the portfolio's coastline. Decadal projections vary by region; tectonics and ice-sheet melt add deep uncertainty.

nodes.name: Tropical Storm Frequency Multiplier
nodes.description: Annual major-storm frequency relative to 2000-2020 baseline. Per-degree warming literature suggests 3-7% increase per °C of warming; significant model disagreement.

nodes.name: Insured Asset Value at Risk
nodes.description: Portion of the portfolio inside FEMA's projected 2035 100-year flood zones. Function of current property location, not climate; lower uncertainty.

nodes.name: Per-Event Loss Severity Factor
nodes.description: Average dollar loss per flood event as fraction of exposed asset value. Historical data from 2010-2024 storm events; assumes existing flood defenses hold.

nodes.name: Probability of >$10M Annual Loss
nodes.description: Probability that the year's total flood-related losses exceed the $10M threshold. Compounding output of physical hazard, exposure, and severity factors.

response.narration: This analysis models annual flood loss exceeding $10M for a coastal property portfolio by 2035. Largest uncertainty is the warming pathway itself — policy ambiguity dominates physical-risk variance through 2035. Sea-level rise carries deep tail uncertainty from ice-sheet behavior. The single most valuable refinement is replacing storm_frequency and sea_level_increment with region-specific projections from a downscaled climate model; generic global numbers add ~30% of the SD here. Note: this is an LLM-drafted model based on common climate-risk benchmarks, not your actual portfolio data. Edit each node to reflect your real exposure and hazard projections before drawing conclusions.
